old_claim.note: Algorithms & Computation Theory provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Web search engines depend on algorithmic crawling, indexing, ranking, and query-processing techniques; Brin and Page describe these components for a large-scale web search engine.

source_shape.source_locator: Abstract and introduction: Google is designed to crawl and index the Web efficiently, use hypertext structure, handle large web indexes, and answer large volumes of queries.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The paper describes a search engine as an algorithmic system for crawling, indexing, ranking, and answering queries over web pages.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports algorithms as an enabling dependency because the cited search-engine architecture is built around algorithmic indexing and ranking methods, not only generic software execution.

invariant_preserved_or_changed: Preserved: algorithms_computation_theory remains an enabling prerequisite. Changed: the edge now has direct search-engine architecture evidence instead of a broad computability paper.
would_reject_if: The Search Engines node is narrowed to a manual web directory with no algorithmic indexing or ranking.
A narrower information_retrieval node is added and replaces the broader algorithms prerequisite.
A source shows early web search engines did not depend on algorithmic query or indexing methods.
short_reason: The retained algorithms edge now cites a search-engine architecture source.
